titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Bradford H. Gray Collection in the History of Social Thought
Gray (Bradford H.) Collection in the History of Social Thought
titlestmt.author: by Beinecke staff

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Monday January 7, 2019 at 04:00

did.unittitle: The Bradford H. Gray Collection in the history of social thought

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The collection consists of approximately 2800 published work--mostly first editions--representing the history of social thought and the development from the early 19th Century through the mid-20th Century of sociology as a scholarly discipline. Included is 17th, 18th, and 19th Century social thought from major and minor figures in England, France, and the United States, examples of pioneering empirical efforts, and a virtually complete collection of first editions (and signed books and pamphlets) by all of the major European and American figures in the history of sociology, including Auguste Comte, Henri de Saint-Simon, Emile Durkheim, Frederic Le Play, Max Weber, Herbert Spencer, Henry Mayhew, Karl Marx, Vilfredo Pareto, Joseph Schumpeter, Georg Simmel, Lewis Henry Morgan, W.E.B. Du Bois, and Thorstein Veblen and the founders of American sociology (William Graham Sumner, Albion Small, Franklin Giddings, Lester Frank Ward, Edward A. Ross, Charles Horton Cooley, and Howard W. Odum). Also included is a complete collection of first editions from the University of Chicago Sociology Series, books from the University of North Carolina Press and a substantial amount of African-Americana.

langmaterial.language: Materials in English, German, and French.

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Collection title devised by cataloger and collector.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Acquired from Bradford H. Gray by gift and purchase on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2012.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The The Bradford H. Gray Collection in the History of Social Thought is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Bradford H. Gray Collection in the History of Social Thought. General Collection. Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections [as they are acquired], and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
The Bradford H. Gray Collection in the history of social thought is described at the title level. Portions of the collection will receive item-level bibliographic control in the future.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in rough original order as shelved by Bradford H. Gray and subsequently packed by Beinecke staff.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection consists of approximately 2800 published work--mostly first editions--representing the history of social thought and the development from the early 19th Century through the mid-20th Century of sociology as a scholarly discipline. Included is 17th, 18th, and 19th Century social thought from major and minor figures in England, France, and the United States, examples of pioneering empirical efforts, and a virtually complete collection of first editions (and signed books and pamphlets) by all of the major European and American figures in the history of sociology, including Auguste Comte, Henri de Saint-Simon, Emile Durkheim, Frederic Le Play, Max Weber, Herbert Spencer, Henry Mayhew, Karl Marx, Vilfredo Pareto, Joseph Schumpeter, Georg Simmel, Lewis Henry Morgan, W.E.B. Du Bois, and Thorstein Veblen and the founders of American sociology (William Graham Sumner, Albion Small, Franklin Giddings, Lester Frank Ward, Edward A. Ross, Charles Horton Cooley, and Howard W. Odum). Also included is a complete collection of first editions from the University of Chicago Sociology Series, books from the University of North Carolina Press and a substantial amount of African-Americana.

did.unittitle: The Negro in Chicago: A Study of Race Relations and a Race Riot. Chicago: University of Chicago Press

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Second Printing
672 pp. Near very good in original red cloth, gilt. With photos and maps. Slightly weak spine.

did.unittitle: Papers on Inter-Racial Problems Communicated to the First Universal Races Congress Held At the University of London July 26-69, 1911. London: P.S. King

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition

485 pp. Very good in original green cloth, with minor library stigmata. With advertisements in rear. Worn corners. Includes Du Bois's paper, "The Negro Race in the United States of America." Also papers by Ferdinand Tonnies, Felix Adler, Israel Zangwill, Franz Boaz, and many others.

did.unittitle: The Correspondence of W.E.B. Du Bois, Volume II Selections, 1934-144. Boston: University of Massachusetts Press

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition
419 pp. Very fine in original brown cloth, gilt, with illustrated dust jacket, very good. A collection of letters to him and his responses, etc.

did.unittitle: Annotated Bibliography of the Published Writings of W.E.B. Du Bois. Millwood, NY: Kraus-Thomson

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First printing

did.unittitle: Book Reviews by W.E.B. Du Bois. Millwood, NY: KTO Press

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First printing

did.unittitle: Studies in Rural Sociology 1931-1943. Stillwater, OK: Oklahoma Agricultural and Mechanical College

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First editions.

This is a compendium of 11 reports on rural life in Oklahoma during the 1930s and 1940s by sociologists at Oklahoma A&M University, now Oklahoma State. It appears to have been bound for the longtime chair of the sociology department, O.D.Duncan, and has his ownership signature several places. Of particular note are two reports authored by William Sewell, then on the faculty at OA&M but later a leading figure in applying quantitative methods to the study of social inequality, and the 62nd president of the American Sociological Association. Particularly notable, therefore, is Sewell's report, published in April 1940, entitled "The Construction and Standardization of a Scale for the Measurement of the Socio-Economic Status of Oklahoma Farm Families." This volume, which is in near fine condition, is unique, and the papers contained are each uncommon or rare.

did.unittitle: Papers on Infant Development, published by the Education Department of the American Social Science Association, January 1882. Contains first printings of letters from A. Bronson Alcott (father of Louisa May Alcott) and Charles Darwin encouraging Mrs. Talbot's "investigations on the mental and bodily development of infants." Also reprints Darwin's "A Biographical Sketch of an Infant," that had been published in 1877 in Mind. The Alcott letter includes descriptions of the development of one of his daughters during her first 78 days. Also included are reprints of articles by Taine and Preyer. The first edition is quite uncommon.

did.unittitle: Papers and Proceedings. Fourteenth Annual Meeting American Sociological Society. Held at Chicago, Ill. December 29-31, 1919. Volume XIV. The Problem of Democracy. Chicago: The University of Chicago Press

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition

295p. Very Good in original cloth. Ownership signature of "C.M. Goethe," who has pasted into the endpapers and title page numerous newspaper clippings , typescripts, and printed programs dealing with a variety of political and contemporary topics. A clipping identifying Goethe as president of the immigration study commission is titled "Alien Situation Abroad Is Told,. Also pasted in are several letters, including one (to Goethe about the movie Blackboard Jungle from and signed by Claire Booth Luce (from the American embassy in Rome) , another from Stanford psychologist Lewis Terman (acknowledging a gift in support of Terman's research on the gifted), and one from Congressman John Box of Texas (dated 1927) regarding issues pertaining to Mexican immigrants. A unique copy of a scarce item.

did.unittitle: Multiple volumes of this social reformist publication, with articles from many major figures of the period. New York: various

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First editions
16 volumes, include two issues of Survey Graphic and issues of Charities and the Commons.
The Survey. Miscellaneous issues from early 20th century. Including articles by major figures, photos by Lewis Hine, and illustrations by Joseph Stella.

Charities and the Commons: A Weekly Journal of Philanthropy and Social Advance. Vol. XVIII, No 23. September 7, 1907. Includes papers by Edward Devine, Emily Balch, and Graham Taylor. Very good copy in original printed wrappers.
Charities and the Commons: The Pittsburgh Survey. Vols. 1 and 2. Vol. XXI January 2 and February 6, 1909. Very good copies in illustrated Wrappers.
Charities and the Commons. March 6, 1909. Devoted to The Pittsburgh Survey. Authors include John Commons. Photos by Lewis Hine. Color Frontispiece, As Men See America, by Joseph Stella.

The Survey. Social Charitable Civic. Vol. XXII. No. 1. April 3, 1909. Includes publications by Jane Addams, Vida, Scudder, Simon Patten, and Graham Taylor. Color frontispiece, Strong Backs Go Into Steel, by Joseph Stella. Very good copy in original printed wrappers.
The Survey: A Journal of Constructive Philanthropy. Vol. XXIV, No. 10. June 4, 1910. Very good. Cover photo by George Hare. Includes Charles A. Ellwood, "Philanthropy and Sociology." Very good copy.
The Survey. Vol. XXV December 3, 1910. Includes "Stage Children" by Jane Addams. Good copy. Missing the front cover, else complete.

The Survey: Social Charitable Civic. Vol. XXVII, No 20. Feb. 17 1912. Includes William H. Welch, "Advantages to a Charitable Hospital of Affiliation with a University Medical School." Near fine in wrappers.

The Survey: A Journal of Constructive Philanthropy. Vol. XXVIII, No. 9. June 1, 1912. Includes Jane Addams's "Votes for Women and Other Votes, and Alexander Wilson, "Field Work in Sociology." Very good copy with cover photo of Birch Bark Slum.
The Survey. Vol. XXXIV. April, 1915 - September, 1915. Nice copy in blue cloth, library bookplate. Entire volume.

Survey Graphic. March 1924. On "Giant Power: Forecasting social changes as sweeping as those ushered in by the Industrial Revolution: Interviews with Herbert Hoover and Henry Ford, plus a dozen articles. Near fine folio in original printed wrappers.
The Survey. Volume LXV, October 1930 - March, 1931. Entire volume in library buckram. Ex-lib.
The Survey. Volume LXVI, April, 1931 - September, 1931. Entire volume in library buckram. Ex-lib.
Survey Graphic: Magazine of Social Interpretation. November 1936. Nice copy in original photographic printed wrappers. Includes "Authority and Freedom Today" by John Dewey.

scopecontent.p: First edition

Four thick volumes. All very good in original brown cloth, gilt, except Volume II (1897), good in original patterned cloth, loose covers. Minor cover foxing on all. Ex-lib from the State Library in Hartford. Begins with the Hand-Book of the American Economic Association and the Eighth Annual Meeting with John Bates Clark's presidential address. These four volumes include, before the two disciplines diverged, papers by sociologists and economists. The authors include, in addition to Clark, Francis A. Walker, Arthur T. Hadley, Charles H. Cooley, early paper by Frederick Winslow Taylor (on Gain-sharing in a piece rate system), and many others. A treasure trove. Rare

did.unittitle: London Labour and the London Poor; A Cyclopaedia of the Condition and Earnings of Those that Will Work, Those that Cannot Work, and Those that Will not Work. Two multi-volume sets, totaling nine volumes. London: George Woodfall

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition

Two sets of this bibliographically complicated book. Set One: Very good in original cloth. Volumes 1-3 are uniformly bound in blue cloth; the second and third volumes do not have their own title pages, but this appears to be as they were bound. The last half of the third volume is "To Correspondents" and consists of printed pages glued to blank sheets. An "Extra Volume" (about Those that Will Not Work) was published in 1862 and is included here in two examples, both in original maroon cloth. The first, though undated, has laid in a very important published sheet from the publisher explaining the irregular publication and the "Answers to Correspondents" section that was in Vol 3. We also include a second copy of the fourth volume, a near fine (though sunbleached) copy of the first edition; published on heavier paper, it is thicker than the first copy. Set #2 is a beautiful near fine set uniformly bound in contemporary 1/4 leather and marbled boards with raised bands and gilt lettering on morocco labels. The first three volumes all have 1861 title pages, but the pagination does not match the cloth set. The fourth volume has 1862 publication date.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First thus.

Two volumes of The Plough, The Loom, and the Anvil, disbound. This appears to be complete, but it is peculiar. The firs volume, Vol. II, No. VI. begins with a letter from Carey (pp. 325-330 beginning Dear Sir) , followed by "Second Letter" (330-32), and then with Vol. II, No VII (paged 380-416, including several wonderful graphs followed by an unpaginated postscript The second unbound volume (Vol. II, No VIII) begins with Chapter Fourth and continues to the end, and is paginated 1-187. Rare

did.unittitle: Miscellaneous Works. Vol. II (Complete in itself). This volume contains 11 pamphlets: Letters to the Hon. Schuyler Colfax; The Resources of the Union: A Lecture; The Public Debt, Local and National: How to Provide for its Discharge etc.; Contraction or Expansion? Repudiation or Resumption? Letters to the Hon. Hugh M'Culloch; The National Bank Amendment Bill; Reconstruction: Industrial, Financial, and Political. Letters to the Hon. Henry Wilson; The Finance Minister, the Currency, and the Public Debt; Shall We Have Peace? Peace Financial, and Peace Political? Letters to the President Elect of the United States; Review of the Report of the Hon. D.A. Wells; Resumption! How it May Profitably Be Brought About; and Review of the Farmer's Question. Philadelphia: Henry Carey Baird

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First thus

Separately paginated. All of the pamphlets appear to be first printings. The book itself is handsomely bound in contemporary 1/4 leather and marbled boards. The individual items and the bound volume are both uncommon.

Elperin and sociologist John Gillin. Notes by Gillen inside rear cover. Uncommon. Giddings's first book, with one of the giants of economics.

363p. Near fine, bright copy in original green cloth. Inscribed "Professor Richmond Mayo-Smith, with the kind regard of the author, Franklin H. Giddings, Feb. 21, 1900." Mayo-Smith was Giddings's colleagues in the emerging Department of Sociology at Columbia University. The book is very uncommon in first edition, especially such a copy as this.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition

302p. Very good bright copy in original cloth. Warm signed inscription to Arthur T. Hadley, President of Yale University. Laid it is a copy of Giddings's "Twenty-five Suggestions to Ladies and Gentlemen who have completed an American College Education nd are Now Pursing Graduate Studies as Candidates for Higher Degrees." These were "provoked by outrages committed in dissertations offered by candidates" for the Ph.D. in one university department (undoubtedly Columbia) in one year."

London: Misc.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Early but mostly later editions.
5 pamphlets bound together. Modern leather spine, contemporary marbled boards.

did.unittitle: Lectures on Science, Philosophy, and Art. 1907-1908. New York: Columbia University Press

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition

Thick volume that is a collection of 21 lectures (each one paginated individually) of lectures on the various disciplines by Columbia University faculty members. They include Anthropology by Franz Boaz, history by James Harvey Robinson, Economics by Henry Rogers Seager, Politics by Charles A. Beard, Ethics by John Dewey, Psychology by Robert S. Woodworth, and Sociology by Franklin Henry Giddings. Near fine copy in original blue cloth.

did.unittitle: Economic Essays Contributed in Honor of John Bates Clark. Published on behalf of the American Economic Association. New York: The Macmillan Company

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition

368p. Fine copy in near fine printed dust wrapper. The volume consists of seventeen essays by former students and colleagues of Clark, most of whom were economists. Authors include James Bonar, Thomas Nixon Carver, John Maurice Clark, Richard T. Ely, Irving Fisher, Edwin Seligman, and Franklin Giddings. Also included is a bibliography of Clark's writings.

did.unittitle: Fugitive Papers of Russell Gordon Smith, with a foreword by Franklin Henry Giddings and an Introduction by Herbert Edwin Hawkes. New York: Columbia University Press

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "New edition" (i.e., second printing)
119p. Near fine in dust jacket. Signature of A.W. Rushmore. Giddings's foreword is about Smith, a budding sociologist who died young after contracting influenza during the first world war.

308p. Near fine bright copy in green cloth, scratch on spine. Except for title page and spine (with Chinese character), this appears to be identical with the first edition of 1922. Ownership signature of sociologist Herbert Lamson, who was in Shanghai at the time.

did.unittitle: Readings in Descriptive and Historical Sociology. New York: The Macmillan Company

Six Volumes of Ward's minor publications, beginning when he was 16 years old. This set is in near fine condition, the only blemish being small paper numerical stickers on the spines (no other library stigmata are present).

did.unittitle: Proceedings of the National Conference on Charities and Correction at the twenty-second Annual Session Held in New Haven, Conn., May 24-30, 1895. Boston: Geo. H. Ellis

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition

559 pp. Very good in original leather (raised bands) and marbled boards. Some rubbing, but lettering on spine remains bright. The editor's preface notes "As was anticipated, much time was given to the science of sociology; and several professors from various colleges and universities read papers or took part in the discussions." The most notable was Franklin Giddings, whose paper was entitled "Is the Term 'Social Class' a Scientific Category?" Among the other authors were Arthur T. Hadley, C.R. Henderson, Charles Loring Brace, and Lillian Wald.

Four volumes in one very thick volume, in library buckram. Library stigmata. Handsome volume, contents near fine.

list.item: Tenth Annual Meeting held at Washington, D.C. December 28-31, 1915: War and Militarism in their Sociological Aspects. Begins with President Edward A. Ross, "War as Determiner" 166p.
Eleventh Annual Meeting held at Columbus, Ohio, December 27-29: The Sociology of Rural Life. Begins with President George E. Vincent's "Countryside and Nation" 233p.

Twelfth Annual Meeting, Held at Philadelphia, PA December 27-29, 1917: Social Control. Begins with President George Elliott Howard's "Ideals as a Factor in the Future Control of International Society." 269p.
Thirteenth Annual Meeting, Held at Richmond, VA. December 27-28, 1918. Sociology of Education. Begins with Charles H. Cooley's "A Primary Culture for Democracy." 224p.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition

124 pp. Very good copy in original boards, spine reinforced with sympathetic cloth taped. Signature of economic historian Sidney Sherwood on title page, and with extensive marginal notations. Unusual copy in that alternating pages are blank, as if to facilitate the taking of notes or making of corrections. This is not reflected in the pagination. The scarce first book by the founder of the sociology department at the University of Chicago and one of the founders of American sociology. Rare.

did.unittitle: The Polish Peasant in Europe and America: Vol. 1: Primary Group Organization . Chicago: University of Chicago Press

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition

526 pp. Very good condition in green cloth. Ex libris. Inner hinge cracked in front. Corners bumped. Publication of this multi-volume landmark in sociology was undertaken by the University of Chicago Press while Thomas was a faculty member. The press canceled the contract after Thomas was forced to leave the university in sex scandal. The whole series was subsequently published by Richard D. Badger of Boston in binding matching the University of Chicago publication. The whole set is uncommon, but the volumes published by the University of Chicago are especially so.

did.unittitle: The Polish Peasant in Europe and America: Vols. I-V. Boston: Richard G. Badger

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition

Five Volumes. Fine condition, in green cloth. Some minor damage to inner hinges in volume I. Minor foxing in final three volumes. On Volumes 1 and 2, the University of Chicago Press designated on the spine has been covered with gilt, with Badger shown on the final three volumes, which are published in cloth that matched the aborted University of Chicago series. Volumes One and Two are the first published with the Badger imprint; Volumes Three to Five are first edition. Complete sets have become rare in trade.

320 pp. Fine condition in wrappers as issued. Young's introduction explains how the original publication in 1921 showed Park and Miller as authors but that Thomas had been the main author whose name was removed after his connection with the University of Chicago was ended after allegations that Thomas had violated the Mann Act. Only in the Pattern Smith reprint is Thomas shown as the lead author.

did.unittitle: The Congo Free State and Congo Belge, Reprinted from The Journal of Race Development Vol. 1, No. 4, April 1911. (Chicago): n.p.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: (383-399 pp). Good condition, lacking wrappers. Somewhat smudged. Very uncommon. "The Journal of Race Development was the first American journal of international relations. It was founded in 1911 by George Hubbard Blakeslee, a historian who taught at Clark University. Despite a name which now suggests a journal devoted to eugenics, the journal, in fact, dealt with a variety of topics connected with politics, foreign affairs and international relations. It was merged with The Journal of International Relations, which in turn was merged with Foreign Affairs in 1922."

did.unittitle: Spencer Tracts. Bound volume of mostly rare pamphlets from Spencer's own library. Includes The Proper Sphere of Government (1843), State-Education Self-Defeating (1851), A Theory of Population deduced from the general law of animal fertility (2852), Over-Legislation (1853), Railway Morals & Railway Policy (1855), The Classification of the Sciences; to which are added reasons for dissenting from the Philosophy of M. Comte (New York 1864), The Classification of the Sciences to which are added reasons for dissenting from the Philosophy of M. Comte (third edition, with a postscript replying to criticisms, and an appendix. 1871). London and New York: various

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: various

Attractive volume with 1/4 leather, gilt spine, and marbled boards. Unique collection of pamphlets, assembled after 1871 (date of last one), bound together in chronological order. The provenance is written on the front free end paper states "This book, from Herbert Spencer's' own Library at Percival Terrace, Brighton, was preserved by his trustees till 1936 when the Trust terminated when the Trustees gave it to me, who had been their clerk from 1904 to 1924, and then Secretary from 1924 to 1936. T.W. Hill." There is also a rubber stamp impression of Spencer's name on an end paper, his usual way of marking ownership. The papers individually are important and rare. The collection is unique.

did.unittitle: Defense of Economy against the Late Mr. Burke. n.p.: none shown

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 47p disbound pamphlet in excellent condition with light foxing. Description from Harrington, 2007: [London: Abraham John Valpy,] 1817, Original. First Edition, from the Pamphleteer, vol. ix, no. 17. The Pamphleteer was an occasional publication of Abraham John Valpy. It brought together such materials as Valpy believed would attract a wide audience: political tracts and Parliamentary speeches, theological essays, pamphlets on economic and social reform issues. Bentham's essay, a criticism of the proposals in Burke's speech of 11 Feb. 1780, was written in 1810 but not published until now. It was one of a number of radical critiques, in this case of economy in government, which Bentham developed around this time but was reluctant to publish, partly for fear of prosecution. Pamphlet, 8vo, 47 pp.

did.unittitle: Report to the Regents of the University upon the articles furnished to the Indian collection. As contained in Third annual report of the Regents of the University on the condition of the state cabinet of natural history, and the historical and antiquarian collection, annexed thereto. Revised edition. Albany, NY: Weed, Parsons & Company

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Revised Edition

8vo, pp. 183; several wood-engraved illustrations in the text; 18 beautiful and striking hand-colored lithograph plates (1 folding) of native American costume and accouterments, 5 plans, and 5 natural history plates (1 folding);. Published originally in wrappers, this copy rebound at some point in brown wrappers with original printed cover laid down. Internally a very nice crisp copy. Besides Morgan's report, the volume contains several other reports, including a description of new species of fossils, by the paleontologist James Hall, and Lewis Beck's report on mineralogy.

did.unittitle: Oeuvres Choises de C.-H. de Saint-Simon precedees C'un Essai sur Asa Doctrine. Bruxelles: : Fr. Van Meenen et Cie, Imprimeurs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First Edition

3 vols. Near find in original printed wrappers. Lithograph portrait and large folding lithograph plate are present. Edited and with an introduction by Charles Lemonnier, one of his most dedicated followers, the set includes Saint-Simon's major works: 1. Lettres d'un habitant de Genève à ses contemporains. Introduction aux travaux scientifiques du dix-neuvième siècle. 2. Mémoire sur la science de l'homme. Travail sur la gravitation universelle. De la réorganisation de la société européenne. Profession de foi du Comte de Saint- Simon au sujet de l'invasion du territoire français par Napoléon Bonaparte. Opinion sur les mesures à prendre contre la coalition de 1815. Extraits de l'Organisateur. Lettres aux jurés. Suite à la brochure: des Bourbons et des Stuarts. 3. Du système industriel (Extraits). Catéchisme des industriels. Opinions littéraires, philosophiques et industrielles. Nouveau Christianisme.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First Edition

65p. Very good copy in modern plain blue wrapper. Attributed to John Sinclair in the NUC, this copy contains indications of same. In what appears to be a 17th century hand, the title page has been modified in ink to insert Sr. John Sinclair Bart in the authorship space, Esq. has been marked out, and a county name has been inserted. The pamphlet is concerned with disproportion in numbers of voters and parliamentarians in different counties and features Sinclair's usual statistical tables.

Tylor (on Quetelet), Thomas Huxley, and Asa Gray.

Eight reports totaling more than 900 pages. They are quite substantive, covering contemporary theories and practices of imprisonment, with illustrations and statistical material. The volume itself is in excellent condition, bound in contemporary leather with a red morocco label, gilt lettering. Illegible early ownership signature on free endpaper.

did.unittitle: Report of the Tenement House Committee as authorized by Chapter 479 of the Laws of 1894. Transmitted to the Legislature January 17, 1895. Albany (NY): James B. Lyon, State Printer

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition

649 pp. Somewhat worn ex-library copy in original green cloth. All library stigmata present, including that this was "cop 2" and several discard stamps. There are two things of special interest. First, the bookplate shows that the book came from "The Sociological Library of Franklin H. Giddings, Columbia University" and that it was "Presented by Professor Giddings," whose reports to the committee "on school attendance, educational opportunities and home life of children of the more crowded tenement districts" are included in the report. The second is that the report is heavily documented with photos, maps, charts, and statistical information. It is a treasure trove of information about tenement life in New York City at the end of the 18th century.

Some 50 pieces of ephemera issued by the disiples of Auguste Comte between between 1858 and 1884 and collected by Dr. Anton Nystrom of Stockholm. Included are Circulares, booklets and flyers of the London Positivist Committee, publications of the Brazilian branch, the first five issues of Den Positivistke Budbararen, Lafitte and Congreve items. Binding very worn and missing about half of the spine, but the contents, slightly yellowed, are very good. Some handwritten content and many annotations. All here is rare.

did.unittitle: Statistics for Sociologists. New York: Henry Holt and Company

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Revised

547 pp + appendix. Fair condition in original brown cloth, extensively mended with green electrical tape. First pages loose. Library stamp from University of North Carolina sociology library. This copy belonged to Hubert T. Blalock as a graduate student, with his signature and address in Chapel Hill. Blalock was central to the statistical transformation of sociology in the mid-20th Century. Extensive notations, many of which are almost certainly Blalock's. Blalock apparently donated this copy (his own) to the library of the sociology department at UNC.

Uncommon (none on ABE 3/09) The Malthusian League originated in July 1877 when the London Dialectical Society organized a defense committee to defend Annie Besant and Charles Bradlaugh in the trial for publishing Charles Knowlton's pro birth control book, "Fruits of Philosophy." These are the first issues of their journal, which was published until 1921.

did.unittitle: Willelmine, Ebauche inachevee Facsimile du manuscrit autographe et texte typographie, precedes d'extraits des ecrits de Clotilde de Vaux et d'August Comte. Paris: Positiviste

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition
150 pp. approx. Pristine in original wrappers, wax paper and board case. Frontispiece photograph of de Vaux. Many of the pages are facsimile handwriting from Comte and de Vaux.

did.unittitle: 2012 Folio 329

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition

8 folio volumes. All very good in original boards. Minor foxing. With tables and charts. Eight volumes published in his lifetime. Compiled and abstracted by David Duncan, Richard Scheppig and James Collier. Two copies of Number VII.

did.unittitle: Descriptive Sociology; Or, groups of sociological facts classified and arranged by Herbert Spencer. Volume X. New York: Williams and Norgate

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

1 folio volume. Very good in original boards. Minor foxing. Still solid. With tables and charts. Eight volumes published in his lifetime. These two published posthumously under instruction left by Spencer. Edited by Henry R. Tedder. Compiled and abstracted by E.T.C. Werner.

301 pp. Very good in original boards. Large atlas folio. "A landmark study of the international 19th century working class and a major contribution to social theory... 36 case studies." Top right cover corner slightly damaged. Rare, only second edition and later in ABE.
